summary: Jeff Atwood responds to Steve Yegge's 11,000-word critique of Agile by arguing that all software development methodologies are essentially religions—belief systems with little empirical proof. Rather than seeing this as problematic, Atwood contends that religious fervor around methodologies is actually beneficial: when developers truly believe in their approach, they become more effective and energized. He critiques Yegge for dismissing Agile as superstition while simultaneously evangelizing Google's methodology with equal fervor. Atwood concludes that the real danger isn't methodology-as-religion, but rather practitioners who don't recognize their own religious devotion to their beliefs.
key_points: Software development has always been a religion—developers band together around shared beliefs (Java vs .NET, static vs dynamic) with no scientific proof
Methodology religions aren't dangerous; they make developers more effective by giving them something to believe in
Steve Yegge criticizes Agile as superstition while being equally religious about Google's methodology
'Staying lightweight' isn't some natural state—most developers are trapped in organizations that don't give them that choice
The only truly dangerous people are the religious nuts who don't realize they are religious nuts
money_quotes: Software development is, and has always been, a religion. We band together into groups of people who believe the same things, with very little basis for proving any of those beliefs.
We may kid ourselves into believing we're 'computer scientists,' but when was the last time you used a hypothesis and a control to prove anything?
There's nothing wrong with a religion that preaches solid engineering.
In order for programmers to be effective, they have to believe in what they're doing. Whether that belief is scientifically and empirically provable is completely irrelevant.
The only truly dangerous people are the religious nuts who don't realize they are religious nuts.
Telling developers to stay lightweight is like telling depressed people to cheer up.
Most developers don't use agile software development. But it's not by choice. They weren't given a choice.
key_insight: Treating software methodologies as religions isn't the problem—the problem is not recognizing that we're all true believers in something.
